speaker: interviewee
text: Again I didn't talk about.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. Let me explain. Let me ask you a question.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like something to eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I want to try--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I have-- I have bagels, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. As soon as we're done, we're going to break. We're gonna have lunch and relax.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- Mira, when we left off at the last tape, you had just told us a story about the day that the Germans came to take away your grandmother. They had called everybody to a place in town to report. You want to pick up with that story again?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called everybody to a place in town, and they told us they're going to check our documents. But it was not true. We gathered. They took the elderly. They took the young ones. They took them away. Later on, we found out that they were taken to a cemetery out of our town, And they were killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find this out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The peasant. The peasants came and told us about. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were left at this point with my mother, faster, brother, and sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you one question. If the ghetto was closed and no one could go in or out except to work, when they let you go to work, how did the peasants bring you this story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Peasants would always somehow manage to come in dressed in different clothes, coming-- coming into the ghetto and telling us what's happening. We always had a contact, a little bit, with the outside world with the peasants. They would come in into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Non-Jewish. That's right. Friendly peasants, friendly peasants. They would bring us in something. They would-- they would risk their lives in a way, but they would come in. So they told us about.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the purpose of their coming there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometime they would smuggle in, you know, like-- like food, information to tell us what's going on. They would come in. So they would come in at that point. [BACKROUND NOISE] Not very often, but they would.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they came into the center of town. You're gathered there. And they took the elderly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they took the elderly. They took the young children. And [SIGHS] we never saw them again. They were gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't affect us well. We were just, you know, grieving for my grandma. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we weren't safe ourselves. It's different when you lead a normal life and something is happening to you. Then, you mourn your departed ones in a normal way. But here, everybody was in danger, and you didn't know what will happen tomorrow to you. So you were so occupied with the fear, with the worries about what will happen to you [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that you have no time of thinking. You don't have time to think. You don't have time to mourn. You just worry about what will go on, what will happen, what will happen tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: You told us earlier that everyone has prepared knapsacks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everyone-- I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had prepared knapsacks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A rumor started to spread around in the ghetto, as I mentioned before, about the Jews being killed in the neighboring towns. So we started to build the hiding places. I'm sorry. I would like to ask you, did I talk about the hiding places before or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Some.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us more.

speaker: interviewee
text: To tell again, OK. So in our house, we had two hiding places. One was a large hiding place, and the other one was a small one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe-- describe it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The-- we used to work on these hiding places at night, to dig out some dirt and bring it to the backyard and build up a nice little hill. So we worked very hard at night, real late at night, and built these hiding places. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was working on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The young people, the-- people who could build those places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this just your family? Was this everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Family and people who lived in our house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and we were hiding away, storing away food and medications in these hiding places. This was a large hiding place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was under-- under the floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: How big was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big place. I mean, you could hide there about-- I would say about 25 people sitting together. And then we had another hiding place, a small hiding place that was just a double wall in a chicken house. And when it happened, when they surrounded our town, I ran with my sister to the hiding place. And we came to the-- we approached the-- the big hiding place and there were so many people, and children crying. So we felt we will hide in this-- we'll take a risk, and we will hide in the small hiding place. So we were hiding in this little place for two nights. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe that place for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was just a double wall and we were in between. And while we were in the hiding place, Germans were chasing a chicken, and we heard it. Can you imagine how we felt knowing that-- that they are all around? And finally, they caught the chicken, and they left. The relief. We were in the hiding place with three men. We never saw them, because when they came in it was dark. And when they-- and when we left the hiding place, we left at night, so we didn't see them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this just an empty space? Was anything in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: An empty space between two walls, a narrow, empty space. And then, when we escaped from the hiding place, we had to do it at night so nobody will see us, nobody will hear us. But I was so clumsy that I stepped on every wire. It made so much noise, but somehow I managed. I escaped. We escaped, and we were trying to-- to get into the forest. When we were hiding in this small place, my parents and my brother were hiding in the large place, the large hiding place. When we were escaping to the labor camp, the-- the ghetto, Dworez, police caught us. And we were with my sister, pretty girls, did not carry anything with us. They told us, you can go to Dworez. Dworez does still exist. But they took away the men. I never saw them. It was night. And we-- we left for Dworez.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, they did not know that we are alive. And we did not know that they are alive. But then we met in Dworez. Can you imagine when we met with my parents? We were holding each other just to make sure that nobody, nobody will ever take us-- take them away from us. Describe your escape for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- when we were in the-- in the little hiding place, we heard some noises outside. We thought that they caught the large hiding place, and we were crying and thinking that we lost our parents. Another thing, when we were in the small hiding place, my father came to this hiding place. And he called us, me and my sister. And they told us the Germans, they trick the people who are in these places, and they come and they call for the people who are hiding in these places, not to answer, never to answer. We did not answer. And that was my father. And he left, and he felt that we are gone. And we thought that they are gone. And then we met in Dworez.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Dworez?

speaker: interviewee
text: The police called us-- caught us on the way. They took away the men. And they gave us directions how to get to the ghetto in Dworez. And that's the way-- it wasn't far. We weren't far from it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe about three kilometers, that means about five miles, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which police caught you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish. The Polish police. Can you imagine? And they-- and they, instead of taking us, the police, instead of killing us, they showed us where to go. And we left, and we came to Dworez.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were traveling with your sister and--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just with my sister. Just with my sister. Not traveling, walking. Walking.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said they took the men away. Which men?

speaker: interviewee
text: The men who were with us in the hiding place. We never saw them. I don't even know how they looked, because when we came in, it was dark, as I said. And when we left that hiding place, it was also during the night, so I don't remember. I don't even know how they looked.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish police caught you at the hiding place?

speaker: interviewee
text: They caught us on the-- no. They caught us on the way from Zdzieciol to Dworez, towards Dworez.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were going with your sister and the men?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the men. And then the police caught us. They took away the men, and they told us how to go to Dworez. And we left for Dworez. That was also a lucky break. You know, when I start to think about it, what they could have-- what they have done. They could've-- they could have taken us together with the men or they could have killed us right there. But instead, they showed us where to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So describe getting to Dworez.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we came to Dworez, and we cried. We felt that we lost our parents. And people who were coming in from Zdzieciol, there were lots of people who were just wandering from our town to Dworez. They told us that our parents are on the way. Can you imagine the joy that we experienced when they came, and we-- we came together?

speaker: interviewer
text: Describes Dworez for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dworez is a little smaller than Zdzieciol, a small town. And then-- and there, Germans were there. And they made the people work for them. Some worked for them at their headquarters, and some were carrying the rocks, building some buildings.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to-- to Dworez, describe what happened next.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to Dworez. They put us into places where the-- the-- the Jews of Dworez lived. And they were so-- I remember that we were-- we were disgust, because the-- the-- the Jews who lived in Dworez, they thought that they'd live forever. And they wouldn't-- they wouldn't let us use this or use that. They knew that we also-- our homes, and we lost everything. We came just-- nothing. Didn't have a thing with us. And so they-- in the beginning, they were not nice to us. But then they realized that they are not going to live forever either. Then while we were in Dworez--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived with some people, families who lived there before. Dworezer, we called them, people who lived in Dworez. So we lived with them. My father, he was the representative of the Jews who arrived in Dworez from Zdzieciol.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe where you met up.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did we meet? We met in-- we met in Dworez. And I-- it's so hard to describe the joy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dworez is not so big. It's a small town. It's like a little village. And we met there. We met. And we-- I-- I remember we were holding each other and being afraid psychologically that somebody will come and just tore them away from us. So we were holding just close to each other so nobody, nobody will ever take anybody from us. But that was for a short time. In Dworez, some men came from the forest to look for a doctor. They didn't find a doctor. Instead, they found me and my sister. They suggested to us to go with them to the forest. My father was not against it. He felt that one day, they will surround the whole thing, Dworez, and they'll kill them all. Everybody will be killed there. So he felt that we should go with the men, and we left, with the idea that we will return and we will bring our parents to the forest. Because logically, we felt that if we stay, we'll all be killed. But if we leave, it's a good possibility that we will go back and bring them to the forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the men? Who were these men?

speaker: interviewee
text: These were two partisans, two partisans who came to Dworez to look for some medical doctors. They didn't find the doctors. They found us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went back. We-- I mean, we went-- at that point, we escaped to the forest. And then they went back, the men, and they brought my parents and my brother to the forest to a family group. And that is the way we escaped to the forest. How far was that from where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember-- excuse me-- I remember when we came out from the ghetto from Dworez. First of all what we did, we took off the yellow patches. It felt good. We were free. We felt good. We are in the fresh air. We can go. We can enjoy. We can-- we can enjoy the freedom in the forest. And we can take revenge for our loved ones.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came to the forest. We were lucky with my sister. Some girls came to the forest, and they were going to join the partisans, and it was hard for them to join. They wouldn't accept them. Somehow, somehow we came, and they accepted us. We joined the-- we joined the partisans. Why wouldn't they accept the others?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I don't know. I don't know. Maybe [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they did not wish to accept them. I don't know why they didn't.  They placed them in family groups together with the elderly people, together with the young ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what a family group--

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what a family group consisted of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The family group consisted of elderly and young ones. And we helped them. We supplied them with food and with medications.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first--

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow--

speaker: interviewer
text: The first day you got to the-- to the forest, tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got to the forest. We found-- the two men, they helped us. We got to the forest, and they accepted us right away. And we belonged to the partisans [NON-ENGLISH]. That means to the partisan group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were about-- I think about 60 people in our group, in our group.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not mainly-- not mainly Jewish people, not mainly. First of all, the leadership, the leadership, they were Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember the names. But I remember that after a while, we started to experience anti-Semitism, to the point where a group was organized, and they decided to leave the group. And that is the time when we left [INAUDIBLE] for Naliboki Pushcha, what I told you before. OK. We were in the forest and those were-- those was [NON-ENGLISH] group.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Naliboki. No. In Ljubezenski Puszcza. Ljubezenski Puszcza. Ljubezenski Puszcza. That means in the woods of Ljubezenski-- Ljubezenski [NON-ENGLISH] forest.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe to me what it was like for you in the forest, what your living conditions were like in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the forest, during the summer, we lived under the trees. The sky, the trees, the pine needle ground was our summer home. The underground hut was our winter home.

speaker: interviewer
text: The underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: The underground, underground we would build something like a-- it was like a hiding place in a way. And that-- that's where we were living during the winter, or we would occupy peasants' homes and live in their homes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were the peasants?

speaker: interviewee
text: With the peasants. With the peasants. With the peasants. Those were friendly peasants, peasants who helped us.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the forest-- let me tell you a little bit about the partisans. In the forest, where did we live in the forest? Where did we get our food, our ammunition, and what was our goal? OK. In the forest, as I said, during the summer, we lived under trees in the forest. During the winter, we would occupy peasants' homes. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Where did we get our food? The food, we got our food from the friendly peasants. They would supply us with food and with ammunition. Where did they get ammunition? The ammunition, they had it hidden from-- hidden before even the First World War. They were hiding, you know, the-- the ammunition in the ground. And some of them we could still use. And the same-- and food, they would also give us, supply us with some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the unfriendly peasants would supply the Germans. They would prepare food orders, and we would stop their places and pick up the prepared food orders and leave receipts. The partisans were here, and they picked up the food. Our goal, we were not interested in getting involved in open battle fights, because we were not equipped or trained for it. But we were interested in getting involved in sabotage acts to interrupt and disrupt the transportation and the communication at the front. Often [BACKROUND NOISE] we would lose our own comrades due to our mistakes. I remember once my husband left on a mission to blow up a train. And they had everything in order, the little hill was camouflaged. And one of the partisans disliked the looks of the little hill, and he tried to smooth it down and it blew up. And we lost three of our best comrades. I'll never forget when they brought the bodies back to our camp. That you never forget. You always-- you always think about it. You picture it in your mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it.

speaker: interviewee
text: They brought-- they brought back-- my husband was on the mission. And they came back to the-- to the-- to the camp with parts of the bodies. It was completely, you know, dismembered. They brought it to the camp. And it was-- it was a horrible, horrible tragedy, because we did it ourselves. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And that-- that was terrible. That was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go on, you mentioned your husband, but you didn't tell us yet about getting married.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'll tell you about my husband. I think I owe it to him to tell you briefly about my husband. My husband was born in Novogrudok. He had a beautiful family. They lived a normal, decent life. He attended the gymnasium in Novogrudok, where all the subjects were taught in Polish. He graduated from the gymnasium and they list Vilna, where he attended the university in Vilna, Stefana Batorego. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He was in Vilna till [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Germans, till-- till the pact that was concluded between Russia and Germany. And then he-- for the same reason that I didn't feel [BACKROUND NOISE] like going back to Vilna, he did not feel like going back to-- to Vilna. So he was in the ghetto in Novogrudok. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the Germans started--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, I didn't know my husband. I was at that time in-- in Zdzieciol. But when he was in Novogrudok, I would like to mention that, he-- when the Germans, when they got into Novogrudok, they came to his place, and they asked for Nochem Scelubski, Norman Shelub. His father came out, and he said that he's Nochim [? Shelubski. ?] And the Germans did not want to accept it. They said, how could it be? He was a young person and you're already an elderly person with gray hair. But he insisted that he is Nochim [? Shelubski. ?] And they took him, and they never saw his father again. And my husband, the future husband-- I didn't know him at that point-- he walked around with guilty feelings, because he felt that he's the cause of his father's death.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he tell you this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Used to tell me that all the time. And he walked around with these guilt feelings. I did not experience the guilt feelings. But he experienced it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you finally meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he had-- one second. He had feelings what they-- what we are constantly reading in these different books about this survivor's guilt feeling. That's what he experienced. He-- he-- he-- at that point, he came-- he was in the ghetto. They formed a ghetto already in Novogrudok. And he escaped to the forest, and he joined a small group of partisans in the forest. I'll tell you how we met.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to break and change-- to change tapes right now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
